headline_pain.summary: Skills lives in spreadsheets. Reporting stops at completion rates. Career pathing isn't connected to learning.

pain_quotes.quote: the only reporting option I have right now on training and hence skills is completion rates. So who has completed what? And that's it.
There's two pieces of it. One is skills being entered into Docebo to push content to the user. And two, there's a whole different skills system that I need for career development.
the skills efforts across Eaton are largely manual. We need to go skills, we should be doing skills at scale and in a more standard and more tech enabled way.

I'm concerned about you can just go on log a load of skills that you may or may not actually have. And then we're at risk of people going 'but look I've got all the skills that you said I would need for this role.'
We know it's not lateral. We want to encourage and enable people to build transferable skills, cross functional skills.
If you have an opening within the organization, you will be able to find the skills you need within the organization before even thinking to go outside for external recruitment.
I think one of the things I'm a huge advocate of is the kind of the two pillar of behavior plus skill. I think the two things together.
pain_quotes.attribution: Jon Kelly · Giacom World Networks · 89 employees

pain_quotes.quote: The career pathing and the succession planning piece needs to be able to talk to the performance management piece.
We came up with a sort of top 10. There were a number of different things but we came up with the top 10 things like project management, communication, skills.

Basically, we want to tell Docebo: James needs at least initially, James needs these X skills based on his job role. And we want that to map to available courses so that Docebo can recommend them automatically.

decision_criteria.criterion: Skills validation beyond self-assessment (manager attestation, spaced retrieval testing)
Career pathing that supports non-linear / transferable skill journeys
Workday / HRIS integration for job-profile alignment
Manager dashboards for team skill visibility

headline_ready_quotes: The only reporting option I have right now is completion rates. That's it.
We want to encourage and enable people to build transferable skills.
Find the skills inside before going outside.
We need to go skills. At scale. Tech-enabled.

competitive_triggers.switch_reason: doesn't support non-linear career pathing, only lateral
skills hierarchy doesn't mirror org structure or role-based development
evaluating against, doesn't connect career pathing to performance management
available but not adopted, no global reporting
skills efforts largely manual, can't scale

ask_yourself_prompts: If a manager validates a skill, do they have any data behind that judgment? Or is it gut feel dressed up?
When you say 'skills', do you mean the same thing as recruiting, performance, and L&D? If not, you have a taxonomy problem before you have a platform problem.
Is your career pathing model built for who already left, or for who you want to keep?
ask_next_vendor_prompts: Show me how a manager validates a peer's skill competency. What's the audit trail?
Show me a non-linear career path that doesn't fit the lateral / vertical box.
Show me how completing a course actually moves the proficiency level. What's the evidence-update flow?
How does your taxonomy stay in sync with our internal job architecture as roles change?

pricing_pattern.common_mistake: Treating skills as an LMS add-on instead of a separate platform-class investment
pricing_pattern.what_they_should_compare: Docebo + 365Talents bundle vs separate skills intelligence platform vs internal-build cost
pricing_pattern.typical_sticker_shock: Skills capability often priced as premium tier; combined with content libraries can stack quickly
pricing_pattern.advice: Get explicit on whether skills lives in HRIS, performance, L&D, or all three. The bill changes by 3-5x depending on the answer.

evaluation_committee.common_pattern: L&D lead + HR business partner + HRIS owner + at least one C-level sponsor for skills strategy

avoid_framing: compliance-first framing (this is talent strategy, not regulatory)
generic competency frameworks without org-specific taxonomy
skills as a feature instead of an architecture decision
implying skills can launch without manager-attestation workflow design
promising AI auto-tagging without describing how the taxonomy is curated
